did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used artificial intelligence, specifically deep learning (DL) convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for image analysis and machine learning (ML) algorithms including decision trees (DT) and support vector machines (SVMs) for prognostic modeling, to diagnose melanoma and predict metastasis risk in early-stage melanoma patients using clinical and dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study involving 196 melanoma patients. It describes the collection of clinical data, dermatoscopic images, and serological/histopathological biomarkers, followed by the development and validation of artificial intelligence algorithms (deep learning and machine learning models like decision trees and SVMs) to diagnose melanoma and predict metastasis risk. The abstract and methods sections explicitly state the study's aim to assess the performance of these AI algorithms on new patient data, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Melanoma Clinical Decision Support System: An Artificial Intelligence-Based Tool to Diagnose and Predict Disease Outcome in Early-Stage Melanoma Patients
brief_summary: This study developed a Melanoma Clinical Decision Support System to improve early diagnosis and predict metastasis risk in 196 early-stage melanoma patients. The diagnostic module utilized deep learning convolutional neural networks on clinical and dermatoscopic images, achieving 84% sensitivity and 69% specificity with dermatoscopy. The prognostic module employed machine learning algorithms, including decision trees and support vector machines, to analyze serological and histopathological biomarkers. Key predictors for metastasis progression included Breslow thickness, BCL-2 expressing lymphocytes, and serum IL-4 and IL-6 levels. The research highlights that decreased serum GM-CSF serves as a marker for poor prognosis in early-stage melanoma. This AI-based tool integrates multimodal data to assist clinicians in personalized patient management and outcome prediction.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary disease entity studied in the clinical cohort of 196 patients. The diagnostic module aimed to classify lesions as melanoma versus nonmelanoma. The prognostic module predicted metastasis risk in early-stage melanoma patients.

Used as a comparator class in the diagnostic classification task (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma). Included in the pre-training dataset from DermNet (over 1600 clinical images) and in the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset.

Used as a comparator class in the diagnostic classification task. Specifically, the dermatoscopy model addressed a twofold classification problem: 'seborrheic keratosis versus others'. Included in the pre-training dataset from DermNet and the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset.

Represents the negative class in the binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. others/nonmelanoma). In the dermatoscopy model, this group included lesions with various artifacts (stickers, pen marks, rulers) and other skin conditions not specified individually.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma diagnosis (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma/seborrheic keratosis) and metastasis prediction (yes/no).
all_task_types.details: The diagnostic module performs binary classification of skin lesions as melanoma or nonmelanoma (including seborrheic keratosis) using clinical and dermatoscopic images via deep learning CNNs. The prognostic module performs binary classification to predict the development of metastasis (yes or no) using machine learning algorithms (Decision Trees and SVMs) on serological and histopathological biomarkers.

all_task_types.target: Disease-free survival prognosis (more or less than 5 years).
all_task_types.details: The prognostic module was designed to predict disease-free survival categorized as more or less than 5 years, which is a binary classification task based on the provided text describing the objectives.

datasets_used.name: DermNet Clinical Images
datasets_used.description: A public database of clinical images obtained from the DermNet initiative, used for pre-training the diagnostic module.

use.training: Used for pre-training the custom deep learning model (initial configuration of network weights).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermatoscopy images from the ISIC 2017 challenge, used for training the second diagnostic model for dermatoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the dermatoscopy image classification model.

datasets_used.name: Cohort of 196 Melanoma Patients
datasets_used.description: A private clinical cohort of 196 patients with early-stage melanoma collected from Cruces University Hospital and Basurto University Hospital, containing demographic data, clinical images, serum biomarkers (IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, IL-17A, IFNγ, GM-CSF, TGFβ, DCD), and histopathological biomarkers (RKIP, PIRIN, BCL2, BCL3, MITF, ANXA5).

use.training: Used for retraining (fine-tuning) the pre-trained clinical image model and splitting into training/validation/test sets for evaluation.
use.validation: Used for validation of the clinical image diagnostic model.
use.testing: Used for testing of the clinical image diagnostic model.
use.other: Used for developing and validating the prognostic module (machine learning models for metastasis and disease-free survival prediction).

datasets_used.name: Early-Stage Melanoma Sub-cohort
datasets_used.description: A subset of the main cohort consisting of patients with stages I and II melanoma according to AJCC criteria.

use.training: Used for developing a specific prognostic model for early-stage melanoma patients.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train the machine learning models and test their ability to predict new data efficiently.
Used in conjunction with 10-fold cross-validation to ensure balanced representation of classes during model training.
To evaluate the performance of the diagnostic and prognostic models, specifically to identify the best cut-off thresholds for diagnosis.
To assess the accuracy of the machine learning models, particularly for comparing different algorithms (e.g., Decision Tree vs. SVM) in predicting metastasis.
To determine optimal sensitivity/specificity trade-offs for the clinical image classification model (e.g., 'sensitivity cut-off', 'sensitivity = specificity cut-off', 'specificity cut-off').

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, and accuracy, with comparisons made based on these performance metrics and cut-off thresholds rather than statistical significance testing.

Clinical Images (Cohort of 196 Patients).metric: Sensitivity and Specificity at different cut-off thresholds
Clinical Images (Cohort of 196 Patients).notes: Performance surpassed previously reported metrics of clinicians' assessments. The model was pre-trained on DermNet and fine-tuned on the 196-patient cohort.

Dermatoscopy Images (ISIC 2017 Challenge.notes: The dataset included images with artifacts such as stickers, pen marks, and rulers. The model addressed a twofold classification problem: melanoma versus others, or seborrheic keratosis versus others.

SVM with ANOVA kernel.notes: Also had a good F1 score

Metastasis Prediction (Cohort of 196 Pat.notes: The dataset was unbalanced with 131 patients without metastasis and 65 metastatic patients. The best model (RapidMiner DT) identified disease-free survival, Breslow index, L-BCL2+, and serum IL-4 levels as relevant attributes. For patients with disease-free survival < 21.5 months, the probability of metastasis was 94.12%.

Early-Stage Melanoma Metastasis Predicti.notes: The dataset was unbalanced with 43 patients with metastasis and 106 patients without metastasis. These models were not able to predict the development of metastasis.

architecture_used.main_model: Two separate modules: 1) Diagnostic module using Deep Learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs); 2) Prognostic module using Machine Learning algorithms including Decision Trees (DT) and Support Vector Machines (SVMs).
architecture_used.pretraining: The diagnostic CNN model was pre-trained on a custom database of over 1600 clinical images (nevus, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis) from the DermNet initiative. It was then retrained (fine-tuned) on images from the cohort of 196 patients. A second diagnostic model for dermatoscopy images was trained using the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset.

diagnostic_module.preprocessing: Data augmentation and imbalance techniques were incorporated. The dermatoscopy model included illumination correction and lesion segmentation procedures.
tasks: Binary classification: Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma (using clinical images)
Twofold classification: Melanoma vs. Others OR Seborrheic Keratosis vs. Others (using dermatoscopy images)

prognostic_module.type: Machine Learning (Decision Trees and Support Vector Machines)
algorithms: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with various kernels: dot, radial, polynomial, neural, and ANOVA

diagnostic_module.fine_tuning_dataset: Cohort of 196 patients (split into training, validation, and test sets)

classification_or_output_head.prognostic_module: Binary classification output (Metastasis: Yes/No; Disease-free survival: >5 years/<5 years)

conclusions: The AI-based Melanoma Clinical Decision Support System effectively improves early diagnosis of melanoma using clinical and dermatoscopic images combined with deep learning algorithms.

The diagnostic module achieved high performance, with a sensitivity of 96.82% and specificity of 75.41% for clinical images, and an average sensitivity of 84% and specificity of 69% for dermatoscopic images.
The diagnostic performance of the AI models surpassed previously reported metrics of clinicians' assessments, demonstrating the value of deep learning analysis of clinical images.

The prognostic module successfully predicted metastasis risk and disease-free survival in early-stage melanoma patients using machine learning algorithms (specifically RapidMiner Decision Trees) on serological and histopathological biomarkers.
Key predictors for metastasis progression identified by the model include Breslow thickness, infiltrating BCL-2 expressing lymphocytes, and serum IL-4 and IL-6 levels.
A decrease in serum GM-CSF was identified as a marker of poor prognosis in patients with early-stage melanomas.
For patients with a disease-free survival of less than 21.5 months, the model predicted a 94.12% probability of having had a metastasis.

The study highlights that macroscopic clinical features are highly relevant for diagnosis, suggesting that lesions should be evaluated as a whole, and that modern deep learning approaches could benefit from hybrid models combining clinical and dermatoscopic information.
The developed tool is a low-cost, effective system to assist clinicians in personalized patient management and outcome prediction for melanoma.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single-center cohort of 196 patients from two Spanish hospitals, limiting external generalizability and introducing potential population bias.

The prognostic dataset for metastasis prediction was significantly unbalanced (131 non-metastatic vs. 65 metastatic patients), which likely contributed to the failure of several models (Random Forest, SVM with radial/neural kernels) to achieve any sensitivity (0%).

The diagnostic model for dermatoscopy images was trained on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which contains images with artifacts (stickers, pen marks, rulers); while the model addressed these, performance may vary in clean clinical settings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; model comparisons were based on performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, F1 score) rather than statistical significance.

The prognostic module's best model (RapidMiner Decision Tree) identified specific biomarkers (Breslow thickness, BCL-2, IL-4, IL-6, GM-CSF) but did not provide confidence intervals for these predictions.

The clinical image diagnostic model was pre-trained on a public dataset (DermNet) and fine-tuned on the study cohort, which may introduce data leakage or overfitting if the pre-training data overlaps with other public benchmarks not explicitly accounted for.

The study claims the AI performance 'surpassed previously reported metrics of clinicians' assessments' but does not provide direct comparative data from clinicians in this specific study, relying on external literature for comparison.

The prognostic model for early-stage melanoma (stages I/II, n=149) failed to predict metastasis with any sensitivity (0%) across tested models, indicating a limitation in predicting outcomes for this specific sub-population.
